content.problemStatement:
An array B=[B1,B2,B3,…,BN]B = [B_1,B_2,B_3,\ldots ,B_N]B=[B1​,B2​,B3​,…,BN​] is called a good array if the following condition holds for every 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N:


BiB_iBi​ is either the maximum or the minimum element in the subarray [B1,B2,…,Bi][B_1,B_2,\ldots,B_i][B1​,B2​,…,Bi​].
You are given an array AAA containing NNN integers. Find the number of its distinct rearrangements that are good.

Two rearrangements BBB and CCC are considered different if there exists an index iii such that Bi≠CiB_i \neq C_iBi​=Ci​.

In particular, A=[1,2,2]A = [1, 2, 2]A=[1,2,2] has three distinct rearrangements: [1,2,2],[2,1,2],[2,2,1][1, 2, 2], [2, 1, 2], [2, 2, 1][1,2,2],[2,1,2],[2,2,1].

Since the answer can be very large, print it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,A3,…,ANA_1,A_2,A_3,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,A3​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of good rearrangements, modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 10610^6106.
